[Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") is a [federal state](/wiki/Federation "Federation") comprising three communities and three regions that are based on four language areas. For each of these subdivision types, the subdivisions together make up the entire country; in other words, the types overlap.

The language areas were established by the [Second Gilson Act](/wiki/History_of_Belgium#The_rise_of_the_federal_state "History of Belgium"), which entered into force on 2 August 1963. The division into language areas was included in the [Belgian Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Belgium "Constitution of Belgium") in 1970.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Through [constitutional reforms](/wiki/State_reform_in_Belgium "State reform in Belgium") in the 1970s and 1980s, [regionalisation](/wiki/Regionalism_(politics) "Regionalism (politics)") of the [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") led to a three-tiered federation: [federal](/wiki/Federalism "Federalism"), regional, and community governments were created, a compromise designed to minimize linguistic, cultural, social, and economic tensions.[[2]](#cite_note-StateStructure-2)

## Schematic overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Communities,_regions,_and_language_areas_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Schematic overview")]

This is a schematic overview of the basic federal structure of Belgium as defined by Title I of the Belgian Constitution.

Each of the entities either have their own parliament and government (for the federal state, the communities and the regions) or their own council and executive college (for provinces and municipalities). The entities in *italics* do not have their own institutions—arrondissements because they are purely administrative; language areas because they merely define the linguistic regime of a municipality; and the Flemish Region because its powers are exercised by the Flemish Community.

The three communities are:

* the (Dutch-speaking) [Flemish Community](/wiki/Flemish_Community "Flemish Community") (*Vlaamse Gemeenschap*)
* the [French Community](/wiki/French_Community_of_Belgium "French Community of Belgium") (*Communauté française*)
* the [German-speaking Community](/wiki/German-speaking_Community_of_Belgium "German-speaking Community of Belgium") (*Deutschsprachige Gemeinschaft*, DG)

The three regions are:

* the [Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") (Brussels; French: *Région de Bruxelles-Capitale*, Dutch: *Brussels Hoofdstedelijk Gewest*)
* the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region") (Flanders; *Vlaams Gewest*)
* the [Walloon Region](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia") (Wallonia, *Région wallonne*, *Wallonische Region*)

The four language areas (as *taalgebieden* in Dutch and *Sprachgebiete* in German), occasionally referred to as linguistic regions (from French *régions linguistiques*), are:

* the Dutch language area
* the French language area
* the German language area
* the bilingual Brussels-Capital area

All these entities have geographical boundaries. The language areas have no offices or powers and exist [de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto") as geographical circumscriptions, serving only to delineate the empowered subdivisions. The institutional communities are thus equally geographically determined. Belgian Communities do *not* officially refer directly to groups of people but rather to specific political, linguistic and cultural competencies of the country.

All Communities thus have a precise and legally established area where they can exercise their competencies: the Flemish Community has legal authority (for its Community competencies) only within the Dutch language area (which coincides with the Flemish Region) and bilingual Brussels-Capital language area (which coincides with the Region by that name); the French Community analogously has powers only within the French language area of the Walloon Region and in the Brussels-Capital Region, and the German Community in the German language area, which is a small part of the province of Liège in the Walloon region, and borders Germany.

The constitutional language areas determine the official languages in their municipalities, as well as the geographical limits of the institutions empowered for specific matters:

Although this would allow for seven parliaments and governments, when the Communities and Regions were created in 1980, Flemish politicians decided to officially merge the Flemish Region into the Flemish Community, with one parliament, one government and one administration, exercising both regional and community competencies, although [Flemish parliamentarians](/wiki/Flemish_Parliament "Flemish Parliament") from the Brussels-Capital Region cannot vote on competencies of the Flemish Region; thus in the Dutch language area a single institutional body of parliament and government is empowered for all except federal and specific municipal matters.[[2]](#cite_note-StateStructure-2)[[a]](#cite_note-OneFlemishBody-3) While the Walloon Region and the French Community have separate parliaments and governments, the Parliament of the French Community draws its members from the French-speaking members of the Walloon Parliament and the Parliament of the Brussels-Capital Region, and ministers of the Walloon Government often serve as ministers in the Government of the French Community as well.

### Subordinate divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Communities,_regions,_and_language_areas_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Subordinate divisions")]

The Flemish Region and the Walloon Region each comprise five **[provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Belgium "Provinces of Belgium")**. The Brussels-Capital Region is not a province, nor does it contain any. The three regions are further subdivided into 581 **[municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Belgium "Municipalities of Belgium")**, which in general consist of several [sub-municipalities](/wiki/Deelgemeente "Deelgemeente"). These sub-municipalities were independent municipalities in the past, but no longer serve an official purpose.

Lesser subnational entities include the intra-municipal districts (which currently only exist in the city of [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp")), the administrative, the electoral and the judicial [arrondissements](/wiki/Arrondissements_of_Belgium "Arrondissements of Belgium"), police districts, as well as the new inter-municipal police zones (lower level than the police districts).

## Competences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Communities,_regions,_and_language_areas_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Competences")]

The Federal State retains a considerable "common heritage". This includes justice, defence ([armed forces](/wiki/Belgian_Armed_Forces "Belgian Armed Forces")), [federal police](/wiki/Federal_Police_(Belgium) "Federal Police (Belgium)"), social security, public debt and other aspects of public finances, [nuclear energy](/wiki/Nuclear_energy_in_Belgium "Nuclear energy in Belgium"), and state-owned companies (such as the [Belgian Railways](/wiki/National_Railway_Company_of_Belgium "National Railway Company of Belgium") which is in fact an exception on [regionalized transport](/wiki/Transport_in_Belgium "Transport in Belgium"); the [Post Office](/wiki/Bpost "Bpost") was federal as well, but is being privatised). The State is responsible for the obligations of Belgium and its federalized institutions towards the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"). It controls substantial parts of public health, home affairs and foreign affairs.[[4]](#cite_note-fedgov1-6)

Communities exercise competences only within linguistically determined geographical boundaries, originally oriented towards the individuals of a community's language: culture (including audiovisual media), education, the use of the relevant language. Extensions to personal matters less directly attributed to the language comprise health policy (curative and preventive medicine) and assistance to individuals (protection of youth, social welfare, aid to families, immigrant assistance services, etc.)[[5]](#cite_note-fedgov2-7)

Regions have authority in fields connected with their territory in the widest meaning of the term, thus relating to the economy, employment, agriculture, [water policy](/wiki/Water_policy "Water policy"), housing, public works, energy, transport, the environment, town and country planning, nature conservation, credit, and foreign trade. They supervise the provinces, municipalities and intercommunal utility companies.[[6]](#cite_note-fedgov3-8)

In several fields, the different levels each have their own say on specificities. On education for instance, the autonomy of the communities neither includes decisions about the compulsory aspect nor sets minimum requirements for awarding qualifications, which remain federal matters.[[4]](#cite_note-fedgov1-6) Each level can be involved in scientific research and international relations associated with its powers.[[5]](#cite_note-fedgov2-7)[[6]](#cite_note-fedgov3-8)

Communities were created in 1970 as "cultural communities" with limited power. In 1980, more power was transferred from the federal state to these entities and they became simply "communities".

Both the Flemish and French Community have jurisdiction over the area of the Brussels-Capital Region. Consequently, they do not have a defined number of inhabitants. The German-speaking Community is the only community with an area over which they have sole jurisdiction as a community. It is located within the Walloon Region, which has even transferred some regional powers to the German-speaking Community with regards to its area.

### Flemish Region

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Communities,_regions,_and_language_areas_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Flemish Region")]

Main article: [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region")

The **Flemish Region** or **Flanders** (Dutch: *Vlaams Gewest* or *Vlaanderen*) occupies the northern part of Belgium. It has a surface area of 13,626 km2 (5,261 sq mi), or 44.4% of Belgium, and is divided into 5 provinces which contain a total of 300 municipalities.

The official language is [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"). French can be used for certain administrative purposes in a dozen particular "[municipalities with language facilities](/wiki/Municipalities_with_language_facilities "Municipalities with language facilities")" around the Brussels-Capital Region and at the border with the [Walloon Region](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia").

The Flemish Region has no institutions on its own. Upon the creation of the provisional regions in 1974, a provisional Flemish Regional Council was installed with [Mechelen](/wiki/Mechelen "Mechelen") as seat. However, with the definitive regions in 1980, its competencies were transferred to the [Flemish Community](/wiki/Flemish_Community "Flemish Community") in order to have unified [Flemish](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") institutions that combine both regional and community competencies, namely the [Flemish Parliament](/wiki/Flemish_Parliament "Flemish Parliament") and [Flemish Government](/wiki/Flemish_Government "Flemish Government") and its administration. Regional laws (called decrees) do however need to mention whether they are applicable to the community, the region or both.

Since the capital of the Flemish Community is Brussels and its institutions have their seats there, it also indirectly serves as seat of government of the Flemish Region, even though the city is not part of it. Additionally, the city of [Mechelen](/wiki/Mechelen "Mechelen") still has a relation to the Flemish Region as seat; it serves as the location for head office during European (and formerly Senate) elections.[[10]](#cite_note-12)

Flanders contains five provinces: [West Flanders](/wiki/West_Flanders "West Flanders"), [East Flanders](/wiki/East_Flanders "East Flanders"), [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp_Province "Antwerp Province"), [Flemish Brabant](/wiki/Flemish_Brabant "Flemish Brabant") and [Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Belgium) "Limburg (Belgium)").

### Brussels-Capital Region

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Communities,_regions,_and_language_areas_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Brussels-Capital Region")]

Main article: [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels")

The **Brussels-Capital Region** (Dutch: *Brussels Hoofdstedelijk Gewest*, French: *Région de Bruxelles-Capitale*, German: *Die Region Brüssel-Hauptstadt*) or **Brussels Region** is centrally located and completely surrounded by the province of [Flemish Brabant](/wiki/Flemish_Brabant "Flemish Brabant") and thus by the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region"). With a surface area of 162.4 km2 (62.7 sq mi), or 0.53% of Belgium, it is the smallest of the three regions. It contains the [City of Brussels](/wiki/City_of_Brussels "City of Brussels"), which acts both as federal and regional capital, and 18 other municipalities. Its official languages are both Dutch and French. In the region ~75% speak French at home and ~25% speak Dutch, although a significant number of people combine these two languages.[[11]](#cite_note-13) The Brussels Capital Region contains only one administrative arrondissement, the [Arrondissement of Brussels-Capital](/wiki/Arrondissement_of_Brussels-Capital "Arrondissement of Brussels-Capital"). However, for juridical purposes, it forms an arrondissement with surrounding [Flemish](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") areas, the arrondissement of Brussels (equivalent in area to the former electoral district of [Brussels-Halle-Vilvoorde](/wiki/Brussels-Halle-Vilvoorde "Brussels-Halle-Vilvoorde")).

Within Brussels, the two Communities have their own institutions that act as "intermediary levels" of government and public service, sitting below the Community institutions, and above the municipal institutions:

* [Flemish Community Commission](/wiki/Flemish_Community_Commission "Flemish Community Commission") (*Vlaamse Gemeenschapscommissie*, VGC) and its administration;
* [French Community Commission](/wiki/French_Community_Commission "French Community Commission") (*Commission communautaire française*, COCOF) and its executive commission and its administration.

In addition to these two, a [Common Community Commission](/wiki/Common_Community_Commission "Common Community Commission") exists which is the entity when the Brussels-Capital Region exercises community powers. In these cases, there are more requirements for the legislative process in order to safeguard the interests of both linguistic communities (de facto the Flemish community).

Since the splitting of the [Province of Brabant](/wiki/Province_of_Brabant "Province of Brabant") in 1995 (into [Flemish Brabant](/wiki/Flemish_Brabant "Flemish Brabant") and [Walloon Brabant](/wiki/Walloon_Brabant "Walloon Brabant")), the Brussels Region does not belong to any of the provinces. Within the Region, most of the provincial competencies are assumed by the Brussels regional institutions and community commissions. Additionally, there is a [governor of Brussels-Capital](/wiki/Governor_of_Brussels-Capital "Governor of Brussels-Capital"), analogously to provinces.

### Walloon Region

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Communities,_regions,_and_language_areas_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Walloon Region")]

Main article: [Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia")

The **Walloon Region** or **Wallonia** ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Région Wallonne* or *Wallonie*) occupies the southern part of Belgium. It has a surface area of 16,901 km2 (6,526 sq mi), or 55.1% of Belgium, and is also divided into 5 provinces which contain a total of 262 municipalities. Its capital is [Namur](/wiki/Namur "Namur").

The official languages are [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") and, only in the nine eastern municipalities that form the German-speaking Community near the German border, [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"). [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") however, may be used for administrative purposes in the four *[municipalities with language facilities](/wiki/Municipalities_with_language_facilities "Municipalities with language facilities")* at the border with Flanders, and German in two such municipalities near the German-speaking Community.

The Walloon Region contains five provinces: [Hainaut](/wiki/Hainaut_Province "Hainaut Province"), [Walloon Brabant](/wiki/Walloon_Brabant "Walloon Brabant"), [Namur](/wiki/Namur_Province "Namur Province"), [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge_Province "Liège Province") and [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg_(Belgium) "Luxembourg (Belgium)").

## Explanatory notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Communities,_regions,_and_language_areas_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Explanatory notes")]

1. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-OneFlemishBody_3-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-OneFlemishBody_3-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-OneFlemishBody_3-2) The Constitution set out seven institutions each of which can have a parliament, government and administration. In fact there are only six such bodies because the Flemish Region merged into the Flemish Community. This single Flemish body thus exercises powers about Community matters in the bilingual area of Brussels-Capital and in the Dutch language area, and about Regional matters only in the latter.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-Lebrun_5-0)** Apart from the municipalities with language facilities for individuals, the French language area has three more municipalities in which the second language in education legally has to be either Dutch or German, whereas in its municipalities without special status this would also allow for English.[[3]](#cite_note-4)
